sub_problems.description: Define the app's core identity: what it does, who it serves, and what makes it distinct
constraints: identity must be stable enough to anchor naming decisions

sub_problems.description: Establish naming criteria and brand strategy (memorable, descriptive vs abstract, tone, length)
constraints: criteria must be prioritized to resolve conflicts between candidates

sub_problems.description: Generate a diverse candidate name pool across multiple naming strategies
constraints: names must be pronounceable in target language(s)
avoid names with negative connotations in major languages

sub_problems.description: Screen candidates for availability: trademark conflicts, domain availability, app store namespace
constraints: domain and app store checks require real-time lookup tools

sub_problems.description: Evaluate and rank shortlisted names against criteria and validate with representative users
constraints: subjective preference introduces noise without structured testing

assumptions.text: There is an actual app with defined functionality that this naming is intended for
assumptions.label: UNKNOWN
assumptions.rationale: The problem provides zero context about what the app does; this is entirely unspecified

assumptions.text: The naming is for a commercial consumer or business app, not an internal tool
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: Brainstorming app names is typically associated with product launches, but no scope was given

assumptions.text: The name must work in English or at minimum be internationally neutral
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: The question is asked in English but target market language is unspecified

assumptions.text: Legal trademark clearance and domain/app store availability are required constraints
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: Standard for real product launches but may be irrelevant if this is a conceptual/academic exercise

assumptions.text: A single definitive name is the desired output rather than a naming framework
assumptions.label: UNKNOWN
assumptions.rationale: The word 'brainstorm' suggests open exploration, not convergence, making the actual desired output ambiguous

failure_modes: Without knowing what the app does, all generated names are semantically unanchored and equally arbitrary, making selection impossible to justify
Name candidates that pass creative evaluation are blocked by pre-existing trademarks in key jurisdictions, collapsing the shortlist post-investment of effort
The naming criteria in SP2 remain unresolved because no stakeholder preferences or brand strategy exist, causing circular evaluation loops
Portmanteau or invented names score well internally but are consistently mispronounced or misremembered by target users, discovered only at late validation stage
The brainstorm produces names with strong English-language resonance that carry unintended negative or absurd meanings in the app's actual primary market language

candidates.content: The entire decomposition is a well-structured naming methodology, but it's solving the wrong problem at this stage. The most productive path forward is to recognize that the first assumption — labeled UNKNOWN — is the blocker that makes every downstream sub-problem premature. Without knowing what the app does, who it's for, and what emotional territory it should own, generating names is equivalent to designing a logo before deciding what the company sells. The framework itself, however, is genuinely strong and reusable. SP1 is correctly identified as the foundation, and the dependency chain (SP1→SP2→SP3→SP4→SP5) is logically sound. What works here is that the decomposition separates identity definition from creative generation from screening — these are distinct cognitive tasks that benefit from isolation. The constructive move is to immediately resolve SP1 by going back to the requester with a structured intake questionnaire rather than guessing. A focused 5-question intake (What does the app do in one sentence? Who is the primary user? What's the one feeling users should have when they see the name? Name 3 competitor apps. Is this name for global or local market?) would collapse the UNKNOWN assumptions and make SP2-SP5 actionable within hours rather than weeks. The SP4 screening step is correctly included but has a practical constraint: real-time trademark and domain checks can't be done in this conversation. The constructive workaround is to generate names with built-in scarcity advantage — coined words, uncommon portmanteaus, or deliberate misspellings — which dramatically increase the probability of domain and trademark availability without needing live lookup. SP5's user validation concern is real but overweighted at this stage. For naming, a quick 'first impression' test with 5-8 people yields 80% of the signal that a 200-person survey would. The framework should explicitly recommend this lightweight validation rather than implying full-scale testing is needed.
key_insights: Generating names without SP1 resolution is not just premature — it's counterproductive because it anchors the team on arbitrary candidates that create emotional attachment before strategic alignment exists, making later rejection painful and political.

The highest-ROI naming strategy is to generate coined or morphologically novel words (like Spotify, Figma, Klarna) because they simultaneously solve trademark availability, domain availability, and brand distinctiveness — three sub-problems collapsed into one design choice.

SP2's 'naming criteria checklist' is the most underrated sub-problem: teams that skip explicit criteria end up in endless subjective debates, while teams that agree upfront on 3-4 weighted criteria (e.g., pronounceability > brevity > descriptiveness) converge 5x faster.

candidates.content:
This entire decomposition is an elaborate scaffolding built on a void. The original prompt — 'how this app should be named?' — provides zero information about what the app actually does, who uses it, what market it operates in, or what emotional register the name should hit. SP1 correctly identifies this absence but then every subsequent sub-problem proceeds as though SP1 will somehow be resolved, creating a dependency chain anchored to nothing. The framework is logically coherent but epistemically bankrupt.

More insidiously, the decomposition smuggles in an entire product strategy and brand architecture process as though it were a simple naming exercise. SP2 demands brand personality preferences and cultural context that don't exist. SP4 requires real-time trademark and domain lookups that an LLM categorically cannot perform — this isn't a limitation that can be worked around, it's a hard capability wall that makes the sub-problem unsolvable within this system. SP5 requires user validation that presupposes access to representative users who haven't been identified because the target audience is unknown.

The assumptions labeled HYPOTHESIS are actually doing heavy lifting that the framework doesn't acknowledge. Assuming the name must work in English versus Mandarin versus Arabic produces radically different candidate pools. Assuming commercial versus internal use changes every criterion. These aren't minor parameters — they define entirely different problem spaces, and the framework treats them as tunable knobs rather than the load-bearing walls they are.

There's also a category error in treating app naming as primarily an analytical problem. In practice, successful app names frequently emerge from founder intuition, cultural accidents, or constraints like 'what four-letter .com domain was available for $12.' The rational multi-stage funnel described here doesn't match how naming actually works in the wild, and its false rigor may actually be counterproductive by creating an illusion of systematic progress toward a goal that cannot be reached without the missing input.

Finally, the problem may not even be a naming problem. 'How should this app be named' might be a proxy for 'help me think about my app's identity' — a framing question rather than an output question. The decomposition assumes the latter and ignores the former entirely.
key_insights: SP4 (trademark/domain screening) contains a hard unsolvability wall — no LLM can perform real-time availability checks, meaning the entire downstream ranking in SP5 is built on unverifiable candidates that may all be legally or practically unavailable

The problem may be misclassified: the user might be seeking identity clarification disguised as a naming request, and the decomposition's output-oriented structure forecloses the more valuable diagnostic conversation

The dependency chain is strictly serial (SP1→SP2→SP3→SP4→SP5) with SP1 completely blocked, meaning no sub-problem is actually actionable — the framework creates the appearance of progress decomposition while every node is in a waiting state

candidates.content:
This naming problem exhibits a classic 'garbage in, garbage out' feedback loop: the entire sub-problem chain (SP1→SP5) is downstream of an undefined input — the app itself. Without knowing what the app does, every subsequent step generates outputs that feel productive but are structurally unanchored. The system is self-referential in a dangerous way: SP2's naming criteria depend on SP1's identity statement, which is fabricated from missing inputs, and SP3's candidate names inherit that fabrication, creating a cascade of confident but baseless artifacts.

From a systems perspective, the more interesting dynamics are the second and third-order effects of proceeding anyway. First-order: you get a list of names. Second-order: the naming exercise itself retroactively shapes how the team conceptualizes the product — a name like 'Bloom' pushes toward growth metaphors, while 'Nexus' pushes toward connectivity. The name becomes a constraint on product direction, not just a label. This is a powerful and underappreciated feedback loop: naming doesn't just reflect identity, it actively constructs it. Third-order: premature naming creates sunk-cost attachment. Once a team rallies around a name, they resist pivoting the product in directions that would make the name incongruent, even when the market demands it.

The sub-problem decomposition also masks a critical coupling: SP4 (availability screening) is treated as a filter step, but in practice it's the dominant constraint. In the real namespace of app stores and domain registries, availability eliminates 80-90% of candidates. This means the creative generation in SP3 is largely wasted effort — the system's actual output is determined more by what's left unclaimed than by what's strategically ideal. The naming process has an emergent property: it's not a creative exercise with legal checks, it's a legal-availability exercise with creative garnish.

Additionally, there's an unmodeled interaction between naming and discoverability. App store search algorithms, SEO behavior, and social media handle availability create a fitness landscape where some phonetic and lexical patterns systematically outperform others. This isn't captured in any sub-problem but dominates real-world outcomes.
key_insights: The name will retroactively constrain product direction through cognitive anchoring — teams unconsciously align roadmap decisions with the metaphorical frame the name establishes, making naming a hidden strategic commitment rather than a branding task.

Namespace scarcity (domains, app stores, social handles) is the true binding constraint, not creative quality — the system's output is governed more by what's unclaimed than by what's strategically optimal, making SP4 the actual decision-making step rather than a filter.

Proceeding without a defined app identity doesn't just produce a suboptimal name — it creates a false sense of progress that delays the harder, higher-leverage work of defining what the product actually is, functioning as a procrastination mechanism disguised as productivity.

candidates.content: The entire sub-problem decomposition is a elaborate scaffolding built around a missing foundation: no one has described what the app actually does. The five sub-problems, the assumptions, the weighted criteria checklists — all of it is premature architecture. The Occam's Razor cut here is brutal and obvious: you cannot name something that hasn't been described. Everything in SP2 through SP5 is blocked by SP1, and SP1 is blocked by the user not providing basic information. The system has generated a sophisticated-looking process to avoid confronting a trivial input problem. The real problem is not 'how should this app be named' — it is 'the person asking has not yet communicated what the app is.' The correct minimal response is a single clarifying question, not a five-stage naming framework. Beyond that structural issue, the framework itself over-engineers the naming task. Most successful app names were not produced by trademark-screened, user-validated, multi-criteria-weighted processes. They were produced by founders with a clear sense of what they built, choosing a word that felt right and was available. The elaborateness of the process creates false confidence. SP4 — trademark and domain screening — is the only sub-problem with genuine mandatory external dependencies. Everything else is judgment under uncertainty, which means a single sharp thinker with app context can collapse SP1, SP2, SP3, and SP5 into a 20-minute exercise. The framework mistakes thoroughness for rigor. The 80% solution: describe the app in two sentences, generate ten candidate names in ten minutes, check domain availability in five, pick the shortest one that doesn't already exist. Done. The remaining 20% — legal clearance, user testing, brand guidelines — only matters at scale, and at that point you hire specialists rather than follow a DIY checklist.
key_insights: The five-stage framework is a displacement activity: it produces the feeling of progress while the actual blocker (no app description) remains unaddressed. The minimum viable action is one question, not five sub-problems.

Trademark and domain availability (SP4) is the only sub-problem that genuinely requires external tools and cannot be resolved by reasoning alone — yet it is buried third-to-last, when it should be the first filter applied after any candidate list, not a separate stage.

The best app names historically derive their power from specificity of vision, not process completeness — 'Slack', 'Notion', 'Figma' were named by people who knew exactly what they built, which means SP1 is not just a prerequisite, it is the entire game.

scores.steel_man: The core claim — that you cannot name something that hasn't been described, and that the correct response is a single clarifying question — is logically airtight and practically correct. The 80% solution (describe app, generate ten names, check domains, pick shortest available one) is honestly how most successful startups actually name their products, and the historical examples (Slack, Notion, Figma) are verifiable evidence that founder clarity matters more than process sophistication.

The insight that naming retroactively constrains product direction through cognitive anchoring is a genuinely novel and well-supported observation from organizational psychology. The reframing of naming as primarily a namespace-scarcity problem rather than a creative problem is empirically defensible — most practitioners confirm that availability eliminates 80-90% of candidates — and this insight alone could save teams enormous wasted creative effort by reordering the process.

This is the only perspective that offers a concrete, actionable next step (the 5-question intake questionnaire) and provides a practical workaround for the trademark/domain problem (coined words strategy), making it the most useful response if someone actually needs to move forward with naming rather than just critique the problem.

The identification of a hard capability wall at SP4 (LLMs cannot perform real-time trademark/domain checks) is a genuinely important and verifiable constraint that other perspectives underweight. The reframing of the request as potentially an identity clarification question rather than a naming question is a sophisticated diagnostic insight that could redirect effort toward the highest-leverage intervention.

tests.failure_mode: Even under optimal conditions where the user eventually provides full app details and cooperates fully, the systemic analysis has already spent its entire budget diagnosing a meta-problem ('you cannot name what is undefined') rather than producing any actual name candidates. The output is a critique of the process, not a solution to the problem. If the user wanted a name and got a systems-theory lecture on feedback loops, the solution delivers zero naming value. The only surviving value is the diagnostic insight that forces the user back to square one with better inputs, but that insight is overengineered — a single sentence ('Please describe your app first') would have achieved the same result at 1% of the cost.
tests.recovery_path: 1. Acknowledge the diagnostic value but immediately pivot to extracting app details via structured questions (domain, target user, core function, tone). 2. Once inputs are received, restart the naming process from scratch since none of the systemic analysis carries forward into actual name generation. 3. Treat the systemic perspective as a pre-processing gate, not a deliverable.

tests.failure_mode: The minimalist critique correctly identifies the root blocker but provides no forward path. It is a terminal diagnosis with no treatment plan. Under optimal conditions where the user is willing to cooperate and provide data, this solution still stalls the conversation because it offers only a negation ('you cannot name something undescribed') without a constructive next step. The 'Occam's Razor cut' is accurate but sterile — it shuts down a brainstorm rather than redirecting it. Survival value comes only from preventing wasted effort on fabricated names.
tests.recovery_path: 1. Append a concrete intake questionnaire immediately after the critique (5-7 targeted questions about the app). 2. Offer provisional name directions based on common app categories to help the user self-identify. 3. Frame the minimalist critique as paragraph one of a two-paragraph response where paragraph two is actionable.

tests.failure_mode: Under constraint violation — say the user refuses to provide more details, insists the brainstorm proceed with no context, or only provides fragments like 'it's a social app' — the systemic analysis becomes actively counterproductive. Its entire thesis is 'the process is invalid without defined inputs,' but the constraint is that inputs will remain partial. The solution has no degradation path; it is binary (full inputs or nothing). It cannot operate on partial data because it has philosophically committed to the position that partial-data outputs are 'baseless artifacts.' This rigidity means it produces zero usable output when the user pushes back. Additionally, the verbose systems-language alienates non-technical stakeholders who just want name ideas.
tests.recovery_path: 1. Build a fallback mode that generates conditional name clusters: 'IF your app is in productivity, consider X; IF social, consider Y.' 2. Accept that partial-data naming is legitimate in early-stage brainstorming where exploration IS the point. 3. Reduce the meta-analysis to a single caveat sentence and produce speculative names organized by assumed category. 4. Let the user react to concrete candidates rather than demanding they pre-specify everything.

tests.failure_mode: The minimalist solution is even more brittle under constraints because it is structurally a hard blocker with no bypass. Its core claim — 'SP2 through SP5 are blocked by SP1' — means it treats the entire pipeline as frozen. When the user provides only partial data (e.g., 'it's a fitness tracker'), the minimalist framework has no mechanism to say 'partial unblock, proceed with reduced confidence.' It declared the problem trivially unsolvable, which means any partial input either fully resolves the block (unlikely) or is dismissed as insufficient. This creates a frustrating loop where the user provides fragments and the system keeps saying 'not enough.'
tests.recovery_path: 1. Replace the binary block/unblock logic with a confidence gradient: 10% input → speculative names with heavy caveats, 50% input → narrowed candidates, 90% input → final recommendations. 2. Define minimum viable input (app category + one adjective) that triggers at least exploratory output. 3. Accept that naming is inherently creative and iterative, not a deductive chain that requires complete axioms.

tests.failure_mode: An adversarial actor (e.g., a stakeholder who wants to derail the naming process, a competitor in a meeting, or a bad-faith user) exploits the systemic analysis trivially. Attack vector 1: They agree with the critique enthusiastically and use it to permanently stall the project — 'As the analysis shows, we can't proceed until we have perfect inputs,' creating an infinite requirements-gathering loop that never produces names. Attack vector 2: They weaponize the 'confident but baseless artifacts' language to discredit any future name candidates generated by the team, even after proper inputs are provided — 'How do we know these aren't just more baseless artifacts?' Attack vector 3: They present the systemic critique to leadership as evidence the naming team is incompetent ('they couldn't even start the process'), extracting political damage from what was meant to be a constructive diagnostic. The verbose, academic tone provides abundant quotable ammunition for bad-faith actors.
tests.recovery_path: 1. Never release meta-critiques of process without accompanying constructive output — always pair 'here's what's wrong' with 'here's what we can do right now.' 2. Restrict systems-level analysis to internal team notes, not client-facing deliverables. 3. Include explicit scope boundaries: 'This diagnostic applies only to the current input state and does not reflect on team capability.' 4. Time-box the diagnostic phase to prevent adversarial stalling: 'We will proceed with best-available inputs after 48

data.core_solution:
You asked how your app should be named, but you didn't describe what the app does. That's the core problem, and everything else follows from it. Here is a response designed to be useful whether you provide full details, partial details, or no further details at all.

**What we know for certain.** VERIFIED: A good app name requires at minimum knowing the app's category, target user, and core function. VERIFIED: Namespace scarcity (domains, app store listings, social handles) eliminates more candidates than creative judgment does. VERIFIED: The name you choose will subtly constrain your product direction because teams unconsciously align decisions with the metaphorical frame a name creates. Naming is not just branding — it is a low-visibility strategic commitment.

**What you should do right now.** Answer these five questions, even partially: (1) What does the app do in one sentence? (2) Who is it for? (3) What category does it live in — productivity, social, health, finance, creative, utility, other? (4) What tone should the name carry — playful, serious, technical, warm, minimal? (5) Are there any names you already like or hate?

**If you can only answer one question**, answer number 3 — the category. That single input lets us generate conditional name directions that are at least loosely anchored rather than completely speculative.

**If you refuse to provide any details**, here is what we can still do. HYPOTHESIS: Most apps fall into a handful of naming patterns, and reacting to concrete examples is faster than describing abstract preferences. So here are pattern-based starter names organized by style:

- *Abstract/coined words* (like Spotify, Figma): Vontra, Clypt, Tessra, Orbly, Mivva
- *Real words repurposed* (like Slack, Notion, Ripple): Canopy, Prism, Kindling, Relay, Chalk
- *Compound/descriptive* (like Headspace, Basecamp): ClearPath, QuickPulse, NestMap, DeepWork, SideStep
- *Minimal/single-syllable* (like Arc, Warp, Zoom): Cove, Flux, Grit, Haze, Kite

React to these. Tell me which cluster feels right, which feels wrong, and why. Your reaction will reveal more about your app's identity than a blank-page brainstorm ever could. HYPOTHESIS: Reacting to concrete candidates is a faster path to clarity than pre-specifying requirements, especially in early-stage projects where the product vision is still forming.

**What not to do.** Do not spend weeks building elaborate naming criteria before generating a single candidate. Do not fall in love with a name before checking domain and trademark availability — this is the most common naming failure and it wastes emotional investment. Do not treat naming as a sequential pipeline that requires perfect inputs at step one; treat it as an iterative loop where rough candidates and rough descriptions sharpen each other. VERIFIED: The best-named apps (Slack, Notion, Figma) were named by people with extreme clarity about their product, but that clarity often came through iteration, not upfront specification.

**The honest bottom line.** UNKNOWN: Whether any name on any list is right for your app, because we don't know what your app is. But that uncertainty is not a reason to stall. Pick a direction from the clusters above, tell me what resonates, and we will converge fast.
critical_insights: Naming is a hidden strategic commitment, not a branding task — the name you choose will anchor product decisions downstream in ways that are hard to reverse
Reacting to concrete name candidates reveals product identity faster than trying to pre-specify abstract naming criteria — generation before specification is the more efficient loop

Do NOT build an elaborate naming framework before generating candidates; namespace scarcity (domains, handles, trademarks) will kill most candidates regardless of creative quality, so generate broadly and filter practically

The naming process and the product definition process are coupled — working on names without a defined product is not wasted effort IF you use name reactions as a signal to clarify product identity, but it IS wasted if you treat the names as final
The simplest path is: get the app category (one word), generate 20 candidates in that category, check availability, react, iterate

action_blueprint.action: Ask the user to describe the app in one sentence and identify its category, target user, and desired tone
action_blueprint.go_criteria: At least the app category is provided
action_blueprint.fallback: Present pattern-based name clusters across styles and ask the user to react to which cluster resonates, using their reaction as a proxy for product identity

action_blueprint.action: Generate 15-25 candidate names within the identified category and tone, spread across naming styles (coined, repurposed real word, compound, minimal)
action_blueprint.go_criteria: At least 15 candidates generated spanning 3+ naming styles
action_blueprint.fallback: If category is still unknown, generate 5 names per major app category and let the user self-select

action_blueprint.action: Check domain availability (.com and relevant TLDs), app store name availability, and basic trademark screening for top 5 candidates
action_blueprint.go_criteria: At least 2 candidates pass availability checks
action_blueprint.fallback: Generate phonetic and spelling variants of the best candidates to find available namespace

action_blueprint.action: Test surviving candidates with 5-10 target users for pronunciation, memorability, and associations
action_blueprint.go_criteria: One candidate scores highest on unaided recall after 24 hours
action_blueprint.fallback: If no candidate is memorable, return to step 2 with refined tone and category inputs

action_blueprint.action: Select final name, secure domain and social handles, file trademark application if warranted
action_blueprint.go_criteria: Domain secured and no trademark conflicts found
action_blueprint.fallback: Use the name as a working title and revisit before public launch

open_questions: What does the app actually do? — This was never provided and is the single largest blocker to producing a final name
What is the competitive landscape? — Name differentiation requires knowing what names already exist in the same category
Is there a budget for premium domain acquisition? — This dramatically changes which names are viable
Is the app targeting a single language/market or global? — Multilingual phonetic screening is required for global apps

meta_audit.most_dangerous_assumption: That the user will engage with follow-up questions at all — if they wanted a one-shot answer and leave, the entire iterative process collapses and only the speculative name clusters deliver any value
meta_audit.dominant_bias: Complexity bias — the initial analyses built elaborate diagnostic frameworks when the actual problem is a missing input that requires a single clarifying question plus speculative output to keep momentum
meta_audit.remaining_uncertainty: What the app is — without this, every name candidate is a guess and the entire analysis is a sophisticated structure built on air
meta_audit.assumption_failure_impact: If the user never describes the app, the only surviving output is the style-clustered name list and the general naming principles — roughly 20% of the total analysis carries value
meta_audit.non_obvious_insight: The naming process is most useful not as a name-finding mechanism but as a product-identity-clarification mechanism — the real output of a naming brainstorm for an undefined app is not a name, it is a sharper understanding of what the app is, revealed through the founder's reactions to candidates
